turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We'll hear arguments next in Dennis v. Sparks and Lynd. Mr. Edmonds, you may proceed whenever you are ready.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The points that petitioner feels are presented to the Court on this petition for certiorari have to do with the scope of his jurisdiction is a person as that term or word is used in Section 1983. Petitioner submits that the resolution of that ques­ tion depends in great part upon the scope of judicial immunity as that concept is applied by this Court. de:fense raises the question what if the judge who sued doesn't raise the offense?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, are you suggesting that it's com­ parable to, analogous to the posture of a United States Senator under the Speech or Debate Clause, that he may not be questioned in any other place?

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: I'm saying, yes, that it's analogous to legislative immunity, congressional immunity under the Speech or Debate Clause if the judge, if the purpose for ques­ tioning the judge or for suing the judge is because of his act or conduct while a judge, acting within his jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But we don't have a speech or debate clause typed for judges, do we?

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's a judicially constructive concept.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: It's a common law of construction; yes, Your Honor. And if the scope of judicial immunity, just going back to Bradley v. Fisher and throw out the cases that have been cited by this Court: Pierson, Embler, more recently discussed in these cases. If that is the rationale, then rhetorically does not the rationale require that judicial immunity apply regardless of whether the judge raises it as a defense? So, in answer to your question, Judge Rehnquist, I would say that it's more than an affirmative defense, as would be a qualified immunity whereon a state officer or a federal officer is asserting that he is immune because of his good faith carrying out of discretionary functions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So what if the judge is served with a complaint in an action such as this and simply lets the time Would you say that that judgment is no good?

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Or he answers — yes.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Your Honor, my response to that is does that not raise the question, or beg the issue, about the underlying rationale for the immunity? Is it up to the judge to decide whether he wants to respond or not, or is it in the public interest that he not respond? Because how does that place a judge --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: As you say, on your submission, it's a matter almost of jurisdiction.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. Because if one judge agrees that, okay, I will participate in this lawsuit, I I'll go in there and we'll straighten this thing out, well, conduct while not carrying out his judicial function, I would agree. But in case he's being sued because of carrying out his judicial function, we know he's a judge.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then it's not jurisdictional-- In fact, if you'd sue him as John Jones and he happens to be a judge, what would happen?

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Well, why am I suing him? For carry­ ing out his judicial function or for --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: For just what he did here, and you didn't raise the constitutionality of that restrictive covenant as it had applied to the other race, assuming the defendant sold? And this Court held, yes. In Griswold v. Connecticut, there the physician had been convicted of the crime of an accessory and an accomplice to third parties having to do with the state's ban on contra­ ceptives. And this Court held that the physician had standing to raise the constitutionality argument that was more directly applicable to the third party -- he's the person for whom the physician had treated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's a matter of -- those cases really turn on standing --

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. rights acts, specifically this Act, 1983. Is it congressional rights in the state court authorities? Here we don't have a case of Mr. Dennis and the other1 parties mugging the respondent on a highway. What we've got here is, the other parties are not before the Court right now having gone to state court, having filed a suit in state court, a temporary injunction having been entered by Judge Carrillo, course of events in the state proceedings by the Court of Appeals.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What would be your analysis if the United States -- a member of the United States Congress, House or Senate, was involved in a conspiracy with a private citizen and then the private citizen is a defendant, but not the Member of Congress? Do you think the Speech or Debate Clause could carry over to the coconspirator who was not protected by the Speech or Debate Clause directly?

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: My answer is yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I must say that here because if it isn't true there it couldn't possibly be true here, could it be?

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: I tend to agree with Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Vicarious immunity is --

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: I am very reluctant to get into the area of using the terms of immunity, I suppose. I can't say that Mr. Dennis had an immunity at common law. I can't say that he had any of the immunities that this Court has held were not abrogated by Congress when they passed the civil rights statute we're dealing with. However, I am saying that the pub­ lic interest, the rationale, the logic behind judicial immunity requires this result. And if we want to call it "vicarious" or "pendent" or "derivative," so be it.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: The rationale requires it, as pointed out by the dissent in the en banc decision before the 5th Circuit. And it's pointed out by others. It requires it because —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The judge is home free?

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: The judge is home free, but to allow to involve whether there was a conspiracy with this judge, ever though he's immune, even though he can't'-be made to partici­ pate, even though he can't be made to testify.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Who said he couldn't testify?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why can't he be made to testify or is that what you said?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where -- what case holds that?

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: There is no case that holds that, Your Honor. It's petitioner's construction that the concept of judicial immunity requires that the judge not participate, just as this Court recently held --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what about -- no, no, a judge isn't immune from criminal indictment on grounds of official immun­ ity.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: That's quite true, Your Honor, but this is a civil case. Do you know why not?

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: No, I don't, Your Honor. John Marshall was sitting in the treason trial and tried to get Jefferson to appear as a witness. And I think they settled it by Jefferson simply furnishing papers, and Jefferson's response was if the President could be subpoenaed from one court to another, he can be prevented from doing his presi­ dential business. But if you say he can't -- that the judge can't even be subpoenaed to testify, it seems to me there's no reason in the policy that Justice White has mentioned, to say that the coconspirators share his immunity.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Between the Burr case and one more recent case, the Courts have held that even the President must respond and be a witness if necessary.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Again, that was a criminal action though, was it not, Your Honor? you get to the civil action area under Section 1983, you're talking about enforcement, about albeit harassment of judges by private persons, the same constitutional or statutory safe­ guards don't apply. But I do agree there is a conflict there that I think is resolved by differing public policy interests.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Edmonds, would there be a state law under the wrongful injunction action?

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: No. No, only the -- Mr. Manges, liability would be limited solely to the bond that he signed. immune? Now, this case, of course, is before Your Honors as -- on the pleadings, on a motion to dismiss, and the

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of course, one of the reasons for the HR. SMITH: How’s that?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This isn't the rationale of the Court of Appeals, the en banc decision of the Court of Appeals?

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: Oh, no sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, you're going to defend that, aren't you?

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: Oh yes, I'm going to address that. The rationale of the Court of Appeals --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I take it you would not agree --

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: I beg your pardon? friend that a coconspirator with a member of Congress would problem, we have to start with the Civil Rights Act. And that of course does not make an exception even of the judge. But the Court, in construing that statute, concluded that the Congress did not intend to abolish the common law immunities that existed at that time, and the immunities did exist for judges. But no immunity existed for those who conspire with judges, under common law. So therefore, the rationale that supports the immunity for the judges does not in any way give any immunity whatever --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if it's that clear, it's rather

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: Okay, this was decided in April of --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right, what other one has changed its mind?

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: Okay. I would say that the Tenth Cir­

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well that isn't a -- that isn't a change

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Sixth is still against you.

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: That's right. But there you end.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right, well, it's about even then, isn't it?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You want to straighten them out, is that it, counsel?

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: I don't think -- amenable to subpoena and to testimony in Texas, and we haven't had any problem with it in Texas. The rules of procedure give ample opportunity for any witness to be protected from harass­ ment, and that would certainly be doubly true with respect to And number two is it doesn't apply to this case person that can testify, sure he ought to testify.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: His immunity is a narrow immunity relating Courts against fallacious and frivolous charges. And if we are allowed to proceed against those who corrupted the judge, then we're still not completely left out in the cold.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well your lawsuit, you say, is not frivolous and I assume that it isn't --

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: That's right. anybody ought to just be able to sit down and think up a bunch of changes to make against a judge, and file suit. I think that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And just so long as he., includes :a private citizen as a coconspirator, he can sue the private citizen -- on to the point that it becomes a nuisance. The trial judge, I think, can determine --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: By dealing with the merits of the case.

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: By dealing with the -- well --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that's exactly what judicial immunity is supposed to prevent at the outset, nip in the bud; you don't

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: -- and this is the first time I've ever had a case --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- you haven't been watching the flow of cases in Appellate courts, either.

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: I realize that there has been a flow of frivolous cases, and you have had to deal with them. But what I --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: With lawyers, and all with lawyers.

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: What I am saying is, as long as the Courts are going to consider the frivolous cases, I don't think any individual has. And so he’s -- the idea that he's not a person is just, it's just a fiction, not even valid legal But now, when you get past that, he has conspired for judicial corruption -- if there's judicial corruption that is one place, where --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, where you have judicial corruption on the part of the judge alone, without -- who's not in cahoots with anybody -- judicial immunity does provide a cover-up, to use your words.

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: Frankly, I don't see anyway arodnd that. If the judge is just corrupt by himself.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, there is no way around that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: By the way, is there a judicial immunity

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: Yes sir. derivative immunity, and the cases that have considered it and decided against it begin -- like this, number one in the plain language of the statute, doesn't provide for deriv­ ative immunity because it says any person --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or for judicial immunity?

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: Or for judicial immunity. But judicial in common law. Now derivative immunity did not exist in common to become immune from liability if he can bribe a judge and make him party to the scheme, he automatically gets immune for his act. To pose that question is almost to answer it, I think.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Couldn't you say the same about judicial immunity, that it almost invites a judge to be corrupt?

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: Right, well, of course, I think that -- I think that is there, and our protection -- and the only protection we have is, that I think we have been very fortu­ what Mr. Edmonds said; that the fact that -- we'll take, simplify it, we've got two parties; the judge and one party, so the one party who bribes the judge finds out that the judge is immune and then you file your conspiracy suit on 1983 and you cannot have a conspiracy with only one party. So with the judge removed, they say, well, he didn't conspire with any state official against whom we could allege a cause of action. Well, of course, that's a fiction, he conspired with him, and as in this case, the injunction was issued as a result of the bribe. It lasted for 31 months, our client abated -- he action, it had the color of state law, everything that was

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: Well, of course, that's involved in it, too --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- infringement by federal courts on state judicial processes and the like?

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: That's right. That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And aren't those expressed in some of the other opinions --

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: Those matters are expressed, yes sir. And the opinions do -- the opinions do sustain it, we point out, that there were pretty frivolous lawsuits, but my posi­ had derivative immunity up until now. So I think you can allow a suit under 1983 when it's meritorious, and if you follow the pleadings requirements of the Seventh Circuit, you on him, the fact that somebody alleged to have bribed him is being sued, --

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: If he could ■ — because he. did get loose. But I think any frivolous lawsuit certainly

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of course, your colleague suggests that he couldn't be called at all. HR. SMITH: Well, that would -- that would be his position, and his preference, I'm sure. But the -- their jobs. They can make them take a deposition by written interrogatories or —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of course, a judge doesn't have any immunity at all for conduct that occurs outside of his judicial office. I mean if a judge hits you with his automobile on some afternoon he's not immune at all,, any more than any other negligent driver is.

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: It's when he gets on the bench and responds. Of course, we don't know where this bribe was given, whether it was out on the ranch or in town, in the cafe, in

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the act -- MR.SMITH: But what he did was when he got on the bench and entered the wrongful injunction in response to that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And he clearly had the authority to enter an injunction?

turns.name: GARLAND F. SMITH
turns.text: He has authority to enter an injunction. enjoined him from the lawful operation of producing oil. They could have enjoined him from pollution but not from operating the property, and that's where it was fatally defec­ tive and that's why the appellate courts ruled that it was an abuse of discretion that he entered it and of course he entered it without taking any evidence to speak of, it was practically on the pleadings. But those were in the case, that's really not too much involved here, but that's what the background is. But anyway, in the extension of immunity to private parties is simply not necessary to preserving the integrity observed that have taken our position in the case. Now, the courts that have taken an opposite position has, it's been notice, talked about these frivolous lawsuits. mentioned. When this case was filed, Judge Carrillo was still sitting as judge of the court in Bigelow County. As it was proceeding, we realized we had a more serious question here on this matter of judicial immunity that we thought when we started out. Frankly, my mind at the beginning just couldn't Judge Carrillo did enter this injunction but that injunction in the course of the procedures and the substantive law that exists in the State of Texas, that injunction was set aside by a Texas court. Sparks and Lynd, Mr. Smith's clients, did not have to go to a federal court to get the Texas courts to do right. The Texas courts resolved the question. He was not left out in the cold. Secondly, they were not left out in the cold because they have pending a lawsuit in a Texas court under Texas law for the wrongful injunction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, I know, but they say that's a briar patch down in Duval County, that they can't get justice.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Well, part of that lawsuit is pending in Nueces County, Your Honor, as was pointed out in our brief. Part of that lawsuit under the Texas venue procedure, the lawsuit as it applies to my client, Mr. Dennis, is pending in Nueces County. but one of the four was a state judge, would you still be here?

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: If one of the four was a state judge?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Well, that's --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that his immunity applies to all the other conspirators, not just a single .one. Is that your posi­ tion? MR. EDMONDS Yes, Your Honor. That's the situation we have here. immunity and your clients come in and claim immunity, based judicial immunity is applicable in 1983 despite the fact that there's nothing .said in that statute about judicial immunity or any immunity.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, do you think that the public interest rationale is meant to kind of put up a facade to the public that all judges are much more honest than normal people'?

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: To use Mr. Smith's term, I guess that would be a fiction that we'd be dealing with, Your Honor. I don't think that the purpose of immunity is to create a fiction. I think the purpose of this immunity that goes back to the King's Bench, hundreds of years ago, is that there was presumption that it does exist. And if we wanted to find that what if we get into a situation where the majority of judges are corrupt? '

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the public interest connection --

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Perhaps public interest will be changed then.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The public interest rationale certainly if the case goes forward against him and there's a judgment against the plaintiffs -- namely that the — that nobody did anything wrong, why, the judge is going to be vindicated, I suppose .

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if the finding is that the conspira­ tors really did conspire with the judge, it doesn't add a whole — so what's the --

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Just the mere fact of filing, the ability to file a lawsuit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well -- oh, I know, but the immunity doesn't protect their buddy from that.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: That's right, that's right. And I have to submit to the court that this isn't as easy a question to answer as Mr. Smith's events. We're not trying to say that there's a black and white side to this. It's a matter of

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even with the explicit, very explicit member of Congress can be sued under 1983 or in many other someone file a complaint with a lot of scurrilous allegations.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, that's right. If we're not talking here about, should the scope of judicial immunity be broad enough to somehow screen out lawsuits as they're being filed as the Clerk's house, I guess the answer there is to respond upon, depend upon responsible journalism that they don't blow a lawsuit like that out of proportion when it's filed, before it's adjudicated. Under the respondent's view of 1983 and the ability to pursue conspirators, these private citizens, I submit that, to follow respondent's view of the case, that the respondent could have gone to federal court immediately at the time that Judge Carrillo entered the temporary injunction, even before The Beaumont Court of Appeals in Texas had a chance to throw it out. Mr. Smith is fortunate in that he can present to the Court such a bad set of facts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he would have very big trouble doing that in the face of the Anti-injunction Act and a few other things.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Under 1983 if we have a conspiracy and if the immunity of that judge does not in effect shield the for damages.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you mean he could -- well, all right. He could probably enjoin the conspirators from con­ tinuing to bribe the judge.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, that is bribery. That's Mr. Smith's term.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That would be enjoining a crime. You. can't enjoin a crime.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Well, maybe I didn't state myself clearly. What I was stating,that 'under the respondent's view of the case, that that just after the trial court stage that he would be entitled under Section 1983 to file a suit for or 8 7 are all immune.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. And that the reason -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You notice I didn't ask you for any support.

turns.name: FINLEY L. EDMONDS
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. As far as the fic­ tion concerning whether a judge, given, this Court's decisions, that judicial immunity is applicable in 1983 cases, to say that, trying to say that a judge is or isn't a person is fic­ tion, that -- one could almost say that judicial immunity itself is a fiction when it comes to applying that to 1983. gate in federal court all states' courts' civil proceedings.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Thank you, gentlemen, the case is submitted. above-entitled matter was submitted.)
